posts.name: Sh@n Agra
posts.cooked:
I am reading about more and more traditional producers becoming less and less interventionalist, and using less and less sulphur. What is the line between this and being a natural wine? At what point does winemaking lead to a mousey, cloudy wine? What are natural producers doing different than that low interventionalists still aren’t doing? Does 0 sulphur make THAT much of a difference than “low” sulphur (and how is ‘low’ even defined?)

Background: I had a poor and expensive experience with natural Cossard wine over the weekend. It turned me off exploring natural red wine in a big way, but then I remembered that a few wines I like are by winemakers who seem to be using similar jargon as natural winemakers (biodynamic, low sulphur, natural yeast, etc.). Regarding the Cossard, it was cloudy and had a mousey feeling on the swallow that made me not want to drink it at all (I scored it relatively low on my scale… nearly undrinkable). The somm said the wine was correct and was nice (FWIW, I struggle to think that was a correct bottle of wine that people would pay 200 euro for - but I don’t really know). Re the Cossard experience… are the best traditional producers using similar thresholds to produce wines that aren’t mousey, cloudy or feral? What is the Cossard doing that differently? Is it JUST the 0 sulphur or something more?

Shan_A:

The somm said the wine was correct and was nice (FWIW, I struggle to think that was a correct bottle of wine that people would pay 200 euro for - but I don’t really know).

Yup…people who have natural wine to sell have a pretty low threshold on what they view as acceptable. This is often their response.
I had a Radikon Rosso that was so volatile/high VA it burnt when you smelled or tasted it, it was painful to smell & drink. I returned it to the retailer & Bryan tasted it and told me that was the way the wine was supposed to be. I had a winemaker run the VA on it and it was over three times the 0.12 gm/100 ml legal limit on VA in redwine. The wine was illegal to sell.
Yet there are natural winemakers who can do it right. The wines of Elisabetta Foradori are one such shining example. The Broc and the Martha Stouman are another example.
For natural winemakers and lovers of natural wines, feeling sanctimonious about drinking a pure wine is part of the experience and leads them to like wines that are horrendously flawed.
Tom

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
I think the right framework for understanding “natural wine” today is that it is an aesthetic movement that sees itself as rebelling against “conventional wine” rather than a defined set of standards. So it’s quite hard to generalize among natural wine producers. Lassaigne is on many natural wine lists, but other than his Coteaux experiments, I’ve never had a flawed Champagne from him.

Wines made today by “conventional winemakers” that are drunk by posters on this forum usually bear little resemblance to what natural wine adherents claim to be rebelling against. DRC is close to making natural wine by many definitions, and by some definitions actually qualifies. Also, because it is an aesthetic, many producers who would be within the definition of natural wine actively would not want the tag because it means something from a marketing perspective. As Selosse has said (while somewhat eliding the question), the practice of growing a monoculture in a field is inherently unnatural, so the entire concept is moot to him.

Many times natural wine is a better shorthand for producers doing “unconventional” things in their region with the grapes the work with or their winemaking practices - by this metric, super tuscans would have been natural wine when they started since they had to declassify.

0 sulphur/zero zero is a smaller subset of the natural wine subculture and can be very dogmatic about nothing added nothing taken away, rather than capable of adhering to the practices while producing wine that tastes good. I’ve had great no sulphur wines, and other times it’s an excuse to put something mousey in bottle, all depends on the producers skill.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
AndyK:

Not a chemist or winemaker, but I’ve read this multiple times. Some old school Italian wines are a good example, they tend to have quite high amounts of VA and age beautifully.

If they aged beautifully with VA, it’s not because of the VA, it’s despite it.

Unless you heavily filter the wine or pasteurize it, if there’s VA, there are almost certainly aceto bacteria in the wine, and they feed on alcohol to make acetic acid. They are not a stabilizing factor!

I’m also pretty sure that older Italian wines were made with a decent amount of sulfur, which helps keep VA in check.

Also, I think CO2 helps stabilize a wine, as I recall.

posts.name: Wes Barton
posts.cooked:
Shan_A:

I am reading about more and more traditional producers becoming less and less interventionalist, and using less and less sulphur. What is the line between this and being a natural wine?

Marketing.

My thought of traditional winemaking is pretty much natural. Ambient fermentation, neutral vessels, no additives other than minimal sulfite. Or, really, no sulfite added, sort of. Producers used sulfur sticks to protect their barrels. Enough to protect the barrels while in storage can be enough to protect the wines. Martin Ray never added sulfite. David Bruce followed for a decade, until he had problems with riper, high pH wines. I’m wondering how many “natural” winemakers use sulfur sticks. Wouldn’t they appeal as the most “natural” way to protect their barrels?

The move away from traditional/natural winemaking was modern innovations in the 20th century. In France. I suspect that began while bringing back the industry from phylloxera. But, the new techniques, including pasteurization, toxic sprays, various additives, reached the United States with Repeal. Large producers implemented that stuff, but others never did.

A push-back against the bad practices was called for. But, we don’t need the confusion, hysteria, misinformation. Exploration on the margins is great, too. But, some proponents of natural wine have lauded an ignorant approach. I know several microbiologists-turned-winemakers. They are the last to worry about native yeasts spoiling a wine, the last to want to do anything that would mask the natural process that allows the grape variety and site to show clearly. That’s not ignorance, that’s curiosity, passion and expertise.

posts.name: Donald Pennet
posts.cooked: The term “natural wine” is a misnomer, and providing a concise, definitive explanation is unfeasible. In Dwight Furrow’s book, “Beauty and the Yeast: A Philosophy of Wine, Life, and Love,” the author describes natural wine as avant-garde. Stagnation occurs in the absence of an avant-garde element in various artistic fields like music, literature, and painting, despite personal preferences. Notably in the Champagne region, although there are few committed natural wine producers, many traditional Champagne makers are adopting ideas from the natural wine movement. For example, Ruppert-Leroy is clearly within the natural wine category. Last year, Emanuel Brochet identified Ruppert-Leroy as his favorite and most influential producer, which was unexpected. When one steps away from the conventional Champagne reviews by The Wine Advocate or Vinous, the most intriguing producers are those who incorporate natural wine concepts to enhance their Champagnes. While natural wine is divisive, it can be viewed as a force for good when recognized as avant-garde, despite existing extremes.

posts.name: David Patte, Sun Break Wines
posts.cooked: It’s pretty easy to be “natural”/no intervention with low pH/high acid wines such as Champagne. Many bacteria spoilage organisms that cause mousiness just will not survive and/or thrive below 3.4pH; and below 3.2pH you can count out spoilage yeasts as well, such as Brett. But, the wines will have an oxidative style if you cellar them (see my TN for Ulysses Collin 2015 Champagne — they did not use SO2 until 2016 according to notes from @William_Kelley and it shows. Still, I enjoyed this wine, but my wife did nail it, saying it was like a great “effervescent sherry”)

posts.name: Donald Pennet
posts.cooked: Natural wines or champagnes, defined by their minimal intervention approach, do not inherently possess an oxidative style. The philosophy of natural wine, rooted in Jules Chauvet’s emphasis on purity and authenticity, allows for a range of taste profiles. While some natural wines might exhibit oxidative characteristics, this comes from specific winemaking decisions such as extended skin contact or open-air fermentation, rather than a foundational feature of natural wines. This is especially notable in the context of low pH/high acid wines like Champagne, where the acidic environment naturally deters spoilage organisms, allowing for less intervention without necessarily leading to oxidative styles. Any assumption that natural wines are intrinsically oxidative likely stems from limited exposure or stereotypes, rather than the core philosophy or technical aspects of natural winemaking.

posts.name: Donald Pennet
posts.cooked:
Since French oenologist Jules Chauvet advocated for natural wine as a minimally intervened quest for taste purity, the term has significantly evolved. Chauvet emphasized the grape’s natural essence, not quirky or avant-garde expressions. Mainstream adoption now risks diluting these principles, reducing natural wine to a marketing tactic. Producers like Odintal exemplify this drift; while innovative, their divisive wines deviate from Chauvet’s original focus. As natural wine gains wider acceptance, the challenge is maintaining its philosophical integrity while adapting to modern interpretations—a dilution often seen in revolutionary movements.

Producers like Brochet, Leclapart, Lancelot, Ruppert-Leroy, and Elise Bechannes aren’t simply following trends. They’re selectively integrating natural winemaking principles to their advantage.

posts.name: David Patte, Sun Break Wines
posts.cooked: It’s true that I should have said “runs the risk of” being oxidative…. Hard to avoid oxygen at bottling, and within the cork itself, let alone disgorgement, regardless of winemaking practices. I was a no sulfur wine maker for two vintages, following as many of the Chauvet principles you mention, but gave up. One vintage was fine (the reds were a little aldehydic, but stable) but not the second (wonderful upon release, but Brett after 1.5 years in bottle….). As for “native yeasts” I was also making for my nephew and we lost eight barrels worth of Cab Franc (due to cold weather/cellar) which was a lot for a micro-négoce house…. Not saying this can’t be done, and those who do it well should be lauded, but it’s very difficult and process/logistics, temp. control, etc, in the winery can be tough obstacles to overcome.

Producers like Brochet, Leclapart, Lancelot, Ruppert-Leroy, and Elise Bechannes aren’t simply following trends. They’re selectively integrating natural winemaking principles to their advantage.

Interesting.

It seems to be that there is a convergence of influences, both on the winemaking side and among consumers.

  • As Andy said, some of this is a rebellion among winemakers against the industrial agricultural practices of the mid-20th century.
  • To some extent it was generational. (Although, in places like Barolo, the sons’ and daughters’ rebellion against things like vineyard spraying sometimes led to barriques and rotofermenters, which were arguably more interventionist.)
  • In France, you also have widespread adherence to some form of biodynamic practices, including the stronger versions from the 19th century that seem downright mystical to the Anglo-Saxon mind.
  • On the consumer side, the natural wine movement falls within the broader desire for organic products – a movement that advanced earlier in Europe than in the U.S. – that fits with concern about the planet and climate change. It’s all part of an attitude: Let’s not mess with nature or rely on synthetic chemicals
  • For younger consumers, high-end, high-prestige wines that people my age could afford a couple of decades ago are now out of reach unless you work in investment banking, a consulting firm or a big law firm. Few natural wines command the price of a premier cru Burgundy (hell, even of a Bourgogne!) or a decent Chateauneuf-du-Pape.
  • Orange wines are part of this picture, too, as younger consumers seek out novel beverages, and orange wines are often made according to natural wine theories.

The wine industry needs more good, ready to drink on release, food friendly wines. Light reds with good complexity. Fun wines. People don’t even know wines can be like that. Something affordable and revelatory in btg programs, rather than heavy dull chores to drink. Good marketing could mainstream this. I seem some on here lauding natural/glou glou wines as addressing that, but they aren’t beyond some elitist niche of hipster kids. How about something like “normal wine”. Normal wine is for drinking on release, collectible wine is for aging. Simple. posts.name: Wes Barton
posts.cooked:
John_Morris:

I know Ridge’s labels say something like “minimum necessary sulfites” or something like that. It seems there are lots of winemakers who are trying to minimize their use of sulfur without ending up with mousiness, or lots of VA or brett. I’d guess there is no hard-and-fast rule. I gather from some of the posts here that how much sulfur you need turns on the pH/acidity and other things in the wine.

Paul’s experiment was with sulfite levels in barrel. Microbes basically have two thresholds in the face of toxins, such as ethanol or sulfite. One is inhibition, the other mortality. The conventional UC Davis advised practice at the time was big doses of SO2. Initially, enough in the must to kill everything, then wait 24 hours for it to dissipate or bind, so you could then inoculate. Then you’d add big doses when racking and bottling and possibly otherwise. I believe Ridge always started natural and only added SO2 after ML. His experiment, as judged by tasting, determined smaller, more frequent adds yielded more complex, enjoyable wine. It was enough to prevent bad stuff from happening, but not so much that good microbes couldn’t do their nifty stuff.

When Paul was first hired at Ridge, he did a deep dive into historic California wines. He found pre-Prohibition wines to be more complex and wonderfully evolved than post-Repeal wines. Pasteurization was probably the biggest factor, as many of those wines were strangely unevolved. I’ve had a few super-mature wines that must’ve been pasteurized. Weirdly youthful with very mature notes well in the background and not much complexity.

The amount of SO2 to avoid mousiness is extremely small.

VA - there’s a lot of factors. It’s a risk with damaged fruit, which sorting well can eliminate. It’s a risk with senescent fruit, fermentation regimes, poor barrel regimes, etc. SO2 can be the best measure, but doesn’t have to be some lazy catch-all big recipe dose to all wines. It could be a nutrient deficiency stressing out your fermentation yeasts. So, doing nutrient adds til you get your vineyard soil healthier.

Brettanomyces take a crazy amount of SO2 to kill. They feed on nutrients. If you have residual sugar or something else they like in a wine, SO2 can help a bit with that time bomb. Meaning brett could take off at some point down the road under proper storage temperature, or quickly if warmer. That’s where minimal interventionists who normally avoid filtering will often wisely choose to sterile filter.

It’s interesting that some producers are known for a predictably desirable level of brett. I’m guessing that’s from a small, consistent nutrient level. A bit comparable to properly bottle fermenting beer, so you’re measuring and adding a controlled amount of fermentable sugar, so your bottles don’t start exploding.

posts.cooked:
From a wine-selling perspective, consumers are often less concerned with natural wine than with producers who support biodiversity and avoid chemical usage. Many consumers seem willing to tolerate imperfections in the wine if these conditions are met. Notably, Rebecca Gibb MW of Vinous gives high scores to what I consider bland Loire producers, while underestimating those inclined toward natural production. Similarly, Essi Avellan MW lacks a nuanced understanding of grower champagnes leaning toward the natural spectrum when she does review them.

Well-known disputes between Jason Wilson and Galloni exemplify differing opinions. I don’t advocate for natural wine, but I find the discourse around it compelling. It’s easy to dismiss it by citing extreme examples, yet many conventionally-produced wines are equally uninspiring. Franck Pascal, a producer who served in the first Iraq War, blames the chemicals in pesticides for his brother’s death due to cancer, stating they share compounds with the ammunition used in war.

Rather than focusing on the flaws in natural wines, perhaps the conversation should shift to the prevalence of cancer in the wine industry. For instance, Bordeaux has recorded high leukemia rates at a local school, attributed to vineyard chemicals. In summary, I’d prefer wines with slight flaws but ethical production over the “perfect” wines that Master of Wines champion, given the health risks involved.

posts.name: Donald Pennet
posts.cooked:
I recall you mentioning some concerns about the variability in Ruppert-Leroy’s champagnes in one of your posts. It seems that you have a particular focus on specific producers in the Champagne region while overlooking others, which might suggest that natural wine doesn’t align with your interests. However, I will answer your question, please excuse the length…

Biodynamic and Organic Practices: At the core of natural wine philosophy is the idea of healthy, balanced vineyards. Both of these practices shun synthetic chemicals in favor of a more holistic approach to viticulture. If you harvest healthy grapes it is much easier to make good wines. The maisons learned this at great cost in 2017.

Minimal Intervention: The ethos of natural winemaking revolves around letting the grape and the terroir speak for themselves, which aligns with the concept of using native yeasts and minimizing additives like sulfur. Terroir is a double edged sword,but the less intervention you have in the cellar the more authentic the wine or champgne

Low to No Dosage: The absence or elimination of added sugar can be definitely be seen as a move towards a more “natural” product, one less manipulated by the winemaker. Again a double edged sword, there have been many tonicky wines but now with the rise of residual sugar in champagnes this is no longer as abig a problem as it was,

Extended Lees Aging: Natural wines often showcase ‘imperfections’ or “flaws” as unique characteristics, similar to the complex flavors that come from extended lees contact.

Use of Amphorae or Concrete Eggs: Traditional and inert fermentation vessels are consistent with the idea of minimizing external influences on the wine’s character. Lahaye speaks of theferemnting wine always being in movement

Natural Malolactic Fermentation: Nicolas Joly speaks of fermentation being like a fever, we should not interfere. Allowing natural bacteria to perform the malolactic fermentation is another form of minimal intervention.

Single Vineyard or Parcel Focus involves producing wine from a specific plot of land, allowing for a concentrated expression of ‘terroir’—the unique combination of soil, climate, and geography. This approach aligns with natural winemaking, which emphasizes minimal intervention and authentic representation of place. By avoiding blending from multiple sites, the wine remains closer to its original state. Such focus usually results in transparent labeling, letting consumers trace the wine’s origins to a specific vineyard. The approach may result in variable quality between vintages, a characteristic embraced in natural winemaking. Additionally, winemakers who concentrate on single vineyards typically adopt sustainable practices, further aligning with the principles of natural winemaking. Overall, Single Vineyard or Parcel Focus is a technique that resonates with the core tenets of the natural wine philosophy.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Pretty skeptical about this. In its origins, natural wine is a winemaking philosophy, not an agronomic approach, and the idea of “healthy, balanced vineyards” is neither here nor there (even if it may be very important to some natural winemakers). I am not going to name-and-shame on an Internet forum, but there are several famous natural winemakers who purchase fruit from vineyards farmed with herbicides to include in their cuvées. What do you know about Chauvet’s farming? By contrast, to take the example of Champagne, producers such as Selosse and Egly abandoned a long time ago or indeed never adopted herbicides. Larmandier Bernier has been farming organically since 1990, long before anyone talked about “natural wine” in Champagne.

As for minimal intervention… it would be better to describe natural winemaking as “non intervention”. Most of the world’s greatest wineries could be called minimal intervention, the “minimal” being the word that does the heavy lifting. Of course, as a wine defined by the presence of an artificially induced secondary fermentation and disgorgement to remove the resulting lees, it is quite funny to hear any Champagne described as minimal intervention, as the interventions here are transformative and unavoidable. True minimal intervention wines are only fizzy by accident.

Organoleptic defects from feral winemaking are not, to me, analogous to the results of extended lees aging. Not all “yeasty smells” are created equal, and the active ingredients are quite different.

You have to go back a long way indeed to describe amphorae as a “traditional” vessel in Champagne. Oak barrels, by contrast, have been in continuous usage since the Celts.

You don’t need to cite Nicolas Joly to convince me of the merits of malo in Champagne, you could just look at the Côte de Beaune (exactly where the folks who started doing malo in their vins clairs looked, by the way). If you have ripe grapes it’s a bit of a moot point, anyway.

As for lieux-dits, I think the inspiration once again is clearly Burgundy, rather than the natural wine movement.

Personally, I have over the years gone pretty deep down the natural wine rabbit hole out of intellectual curiosity and because sometimes the bottles could be very compelling. I have visited many, mainly in the Beaujolais and Jura. And I have even produced a wine for my own amusement with 20 ppm total sulfites and zero free that is closing in on a year in bottle without any flaws.

But I can’t honestly say, thinking about it now, that I have learned anything of interest from a self-styled natural winemaker. One is more likely to be shocked by their complete insouciance with regards to sanitation: sticky wineries, filled with fruit flies, with equipment scattered over the floor, empty barrels sitting outside in the sun, unwashed wine glasses and plates stacked in the sink… What’s more, in the extreme vintages we increasingly have in France, you can get away with less and less. The “pruine” of ambient yeasts on the grape skin only establishes itself from véraison onwards, and is hugely defined by the climate of that period; heat waves distort the microbiome, encouraging all sorts of nasty stuff. Far from the resulting flaws being proof of authenticity, in my view they occlude the expression of terroir.

Having come into wine from the natural side and learned about “classical” wine from there as I grew into it, I do feel like I originally thought of natural wine as “put grapes in a barrel/vessel and let the hanger-on yeasts ferment it and then pour it into bottles.”

I learned that natural yeasts is a feature of many classical wines too, so while I think there’s a camp of “we don’t use lab yeast strains or Purple or chaptalize” — which when describing to a non wine person all sound like cheating (and if I think about it too hard, I agree)— I wonder also where things like punch downs and pump overs and battonage fit. They certainly don’t seem like minimal interventions, but I rarely know.

One of the reasons I struggle with the natural wine movement is that “taking what nature gives me” isn’t how they make wine. Having spoken to many winemakers (including a good number that the natural wine movement claims as its own), even those who use limited intervention make a lot of decisions in a vintage: how to till, when to spray with copper, how much cover crop to plant (if at all), how much to prune, green harvesting, canopy management, trellising, when to harvest, whether to sort in the vineyard or in the winery, whether to use stems (or how much), and that’s before they actually do anything one would technically consider “winemaking”. Then, as you said, there are decisions about pumpovers, punch downs, battonage, racking, controlling temperature during fermentation (listen to some of Christophe Roumier’s interviews on the subject), the choice of oak toasting, how long to age in oak, etc. etc.

None of this is “what nature gives you”, it’s working in concert with nature, but moving the end product in a certain direction.

Dear William,

So much passion in a text, something one is not used to from you. I think there are two issues which need to be addressed: the semantic one and the agronomic one.

Natural wine and minimal intervention are ambiguous terms, and seeing the confusion or multiple interpretations only adds to the Tower of Babel syndrome associated with this form of winemaking. The ambiguity might actually contribute to the richness and diversity of interpretations, fostering innovation and debate in the field. I am not an advocate of natural winemaking in its extreme form, but I maintain that it is the avant-garde of winemaking, and its influences are manifesting in many regions in many forms. Here, avant-garde refers to practices that push the boundaries of traditional winemaking, encouraging experimentation and fostering new methods.

To get into a debate about what minimal intervention is, is pointless as, like natural wine, it is not codified or certified.

I am a bit disappointed that you chose to list the extremes of natural winemaking. Imagine if we went the other way and listed the additives and pesticides that are not declared on the bottles, etc., used by conventional winemakers, the damage they do to the environment, the destruction of biodiversity, and the homogeneous mess they produce.

I will stick to Champagne and especially the example of Ruppert-Leroy. They lived in the Jura and were mentored by Overnoy. Now, what you list above cannot be said of Overnoy, and I am sure you must agree that Overnoy is a paragon in the natural wine-making scene. It’s important to acknowledge that Overnoy’s exemplary methods are not universally adopted, but his influence remains significant, potentially serving as a gold standard.

Ruppert-Leroy, under the guidance of Overnoy, implemented practices in near-fanatical adherence. The Champagnes are decisive; you do not review them like you do not review many of the great Champagne producers of the region.By ‘decisive,’ I mean that the Champagnes have a distinct, unequivocal character that stands apart from mass-produced options

I agree, it is true that hot vintages can affect the microbiome. If the producer takes a holistic approach, then they are better equipped to offset the consequences of the hotter vintages we are seeing. Yet natural yeasts are better equipped to local conditions than commercial yeasts; this is uncontradictable. Then the idea of a flaw being a flaw; perhaps a flaw is a true reflection of terroir and the vintage. It is my belief that consumers who are more open-minded and care about biodiversity accept what some would call flaws as an absolute expression of terroir.

I think one thing that should also be highlighted is that producers going down the natural wine route produce more complex and flavorful wines and Champagnes; this is an ineluctable truth

posts.name: David Patte, Sun Break Wines
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

And I have even produced a wine for my own amusement with 20 ppm total sulfites and zero free that is closing in on a year in bottle without any flaws.

I did this this during two vintages. One has been fine, the second started showing Brett. at 1 to 1.5yrs in bottle. Lots of factors at play on the second one that may have been the cause (I had newly acquired used barrels from a colleague; and the cellar was cold and the ferments took a long time to kick off— could this have given Brett. a start?). On this topic I know you get this, but for others, it’s important to remember that SO2 additions depend on pH for its effectiveness in controlling spoilage organisms. For example, a Pinot Noir requires less SO2 than a Cab/Merlot wine in general (there are exceptions of course) simply due to the lower pH in Pinot compared a Bordeaux or Napa Cab or blend. But here’s something new. We have new research showing that under a low SO2 regime, Brett. can evolve within one vintage in the cellar to a strain that is resistant to SO2– and at that point, it would be very difficult to recover from this decision. Here’s the link to the research.

I did call myself a natural winemaker the first 2 years, but no longer do so. Too much bad winemaking in that category and I don’t want to be associated with that. I want to make wines that can last. I still will say low intervention at times, but even moving away from that recently. I still follow the same principles, natural yeast etc, but will filter and protect with sulphur.

But perhaps a more important business realisation is startin to manifest: customers who buy natural wines and are dogmatic about that, are also a) not loyal and b) not volume customers. So if you’re planning on catering to these customers as a winery, just know that they’re fickle and will move on in a heartbeat when the next flavor-of-the-month cool Azerbaijan amphora winemaker comes along.

Natural wine is just bad business, bottom line.

Sorry, Greg. I ran out of tokens for ChatGPT, which explains the delayed response.

One wonders why the ambiguous term “natural wine” incites such reactionary views. After all, nobody is forced to drink it; there’s plenty of highly-rated, yet uninspiring wine available. What we’re observing is the difference between perception and reality, and the ambiguity of what is considered authentic leads to such polarization.

As someone who sells wine, predominantly champagne, I cannot afford to take such a stance. The wine and, especially, the champagne market are highly segmented. Critics may reject natural wine, but demand exists. This summer, the best-selling champagnes were from Chavost and Elise Dechannes. Among the big names, Ruppert-Leroy always sells out first. Marguet is divisive, but in essence, he’s a brilliant winemaker.

Producers like Philippe Lancelot, Leclapart, and Emanuel Brochet are geniuses. Unfortunately for me, a producer like Selosse has become obsolete; I can find no justification for such high prices.

Where I want to go is to discuss the authenticity of critics. This isn’t about criticizing William Kelley specifically but the critic scene at large. Consumers buy, enjoy, and repurchase wines and champagnes with a natural leaning, yet critics reject them. The issue could be framed as expertise versus popularity, but I believe it goes deeper. I chastise William Kelley for distorting reviews by focusing on a very small percentage of producers. This critique can be leveled at any number of critics, including Galloni, Evallan MW, and, most tragically, Anne Krebiehl MW, who will review champagne for Vinous. Thankfully, the MWs, or ‘mercenaries of wine’ as I like to think of them, have no real influence.

Returning to the authenticity of critics, if they reject natural wine, that essentially means they endorse commercial wine, with all its consequences. Sadly, one must question the extent to which financial bias plays a role. The Wine Advocate has links to producers selling wine, Galloni sells reviews upfront, and MWs are like guns for hire. This also unfortunately reveals a lack of understanding for the cultural aspects of winemaking, which in turn results in a lack of objectivity. If a critic cannot appreciate diversity and assess objectively, this undoubtedly highlights their limitations.

Natural wine and minimal intervention, despite their ambiguities, are here to stay. While they may have started as philosophies, like Steiner never specifically discussing biodynamics in the context of wine, his theories have had wide-reaching implications in the winemaking scene, just like the theories behind natural winemaking.

If one delves into the history of single vineyards, it’s withoutdoubt that Burgundy plays a significant role, as do the Rheingau, Alsace, and the Loire. To be pedantic about these points is to miss the essence of the discussion. The concept of the single vineyard is a modern one. In medieval times, certain vineyards were considered superior to others. A prime example that encapsulates medieval thinking is the vineyards of Maximin Grünhäuser: Abtsberg (for the abbot), Herrenberg (for the nobles), and Bruderberg (for the lower castes). For this reason, we must be cautious in defining single vineyards within the modern context, as our contemporary understanding bears little resemblance to medieval perspectives.

We could engage in a fascinating dialogue about why the Cistercian monks, who had a significant impact on the Champagne region, chose to blend rather than produce single-vineyard wines. Although Overnoy and Jules Chauvet did not explicitly address single vineyards, these are considered the ultimate expression of terroir and are therefore relevant to natural wine. Terroir is another term fraught with ambiguity. If we accept that terroir represents the unique characteristics of a place, a grape, and a vintage, we may have to make exceptions for vintners like Marcel Deiss who produce field blends. The question then arises: how should this be best expressed in the cellar? What constitutes minimal intervention remains undefined, yet the influential work of Overnoy provides some clarity.

No synthetic chemicals, natural fermentation, no additives such as sulfites, gentle extraction, no fining or filtration, and perhaps most importantly, transparency. While one could argue that this approach is philosophical, the influence of Overnoy is undeniable. Returning to the topic of single vineyards, although neither Chauvet nor Overnoy explicitly discuss this, the natural progression is to consider the importance of single vineyards in the expression of terroir.

posts.name: Lars Carlberg
posts.cooked:
The concept of the single vineyard is a modern one. In medieval times, certain vineyards were considered superior to others. A prime example that encapsulates medieval thinking is the vineyards of Maximin Grünhäuser: Abtsberg (for the abbot), Herrenberg (for the nobles), and Bruderberg (for the lower castes). For this reason, we must be cautious in defining single vineyards within the modern context, as our contemporary understanding bears little resemblance to medieval perspectives.

On a side note, I have never seen a historical reference to Abtsberg at Maximin Grünhaus. Herrenberg and Bruderberg were the only two sites listed in the 19th century. In fact, Maximiner Grünhäuser Herrenberg matches what was later designated as Maximin Grünhäuser Abtsberg, thus the inscription “Maximiner-Grünhäuser-Herrenberg” on the 1873 stone gate at the foot of that slope.

posts.cooked: In Germany in the late 19th and early 20th centuries, the term “natural wine” commonly referred to wine that originated from musts to which neither sugar nor water had been added. Starting in 1971, it was renamed a quality wine with distinction, Qualitätswein mit Prädikat in German, which includes Kabinett, Spätlese, Auslese, and so on. Qualitätswein mit Prädikat was later shortened to Prädikatswein.

Yet natural yeasts are better equipped to local conditions than commercial yeasts; this is uncontradictable.

The standard commercial yeasts are natural yeast. They’re just isolated. There is no logic to make a blanket statement that the ever changing population on the grapes’ surfaces plus the yeasts that incidentally make it into fermenters are inherently “better equipped”. As I noted prior, my experience with many many hundreds of ambient fermentations, most go just fine. Some, often consistently from a given vineyard, will not turn out fine if you don’t inoculate. Many reasons. Yeast-derived off aromas. Low tolerance for ethanol. Inability to handle the poor nutrient must from the same “local conditions” those yeasts thrived in. Inability to compete with an unfortunate yeast that’s “native” to the winemaking facility.

posts.name: David Patte, Sun Break Wines
posts.cooked:
Exactly. “Commercial” wine fermentation yeast were isolated in labs (generally in France is my impression) and trials were run to see how they perform, what flavors they impart, etc. (fermentation yeasts are important for flavor profiles, and thus they are linked to the expression of terroir). Then they are cultured and packaged for sale. Classic breeding, no GMO or other “unnatural” crazy stuff. It is very similar to purchasing baker’s yeast, sour dough starter, etc. The cool thing is that there is a huge diversity of these yeasts, and a vineyard may have unique strains which can impact a unique flavor profile— or they could be terrible performers (strong start, poor finish; or the reverse). With advances in PCR/genetic testing, we can now send samples to labs that can id the strains to see if they are unique. These companies will then culture your “native”/unique species and wine makers can start testing to see how the perform, what flavors they emphasize, etc.

But yes, the other side of the coin is that there are hundreds, if not thousands, of non-fermentation species that can cause a fermentation to really go sideways (to say the least). The most common are yeast species from the Hanseniaspora genera (which used to be named Kloeckera), and the Candida genera, and a host of bacteria species from the acetobacter genera, etc., etc. These yeast and bacteria species will cause a tremendous amount of acetic acid formation very quickly, which is terrible in and of itself, but then AA can be metabolized into ethyl acetate. The EA fingerprint is very common in musts that are slow to start their saccharomyces c. alcoholic fermentation. This is also a risk with cold soak…

But there are tons of winemakers, domaines and estates who have somehow found or acquired great species and do not need to rely on inoculations. Still, another common and time-honored technique is to use a “pied de cuve”, or starter culture, from your estate or vineyard grapes a few days prior to harvest and use that to get your fermentations going. This will also help ensure that you are using a vineyard species, not a winery-equipment-barrel based species which may have been derived from elsewhere. Especially if you culture your pied-de-cube at home, for example.

Anyway, it’s a complex topic!

D_Pennet:

Not the same as your prior wording, but this statement shows a very shallow misunderstanding of terroir. The soil biome plays no role? The grape surface biome plays no role on flavor/aroma compound and precursor compound development in the grapes? Aromatic compounds from surrounding flora don’t contribute to terroir?

One of my points you ignored was ambient yeasts in the winery can take control. They can do that right from the start and completely sideline yeasts that came in on the grapes. Yeasts that come in on the grape surfaces themselves are incapable of finishing a ferment. Most of them have a very low ethanol tolerance. A typical ambient ferment has the wild stuff doing their things for the first phase and dying off by maybe 2-4% ABV. Gone. Something else took over that wasn’t from the grape surfaces. That dominates for the next phase, then something else takes over. Depending what’s in or gets into the must, the nutrients, the micro and macro toxins, whatever other factors that, in the ever changing environment, favor some strains while inhibiting others.

So, what if a winery formerly or currently houses cider production? Your ignorance based approach says do nothing because all the yeast came from the vineyard. The wines would sure taste like “natural wines”, but that wouldn’t be from terroir.

One of the major issues in the old natural wine debates was the glaring exceptions allowed that were specific to challenges of a given producer/region, stated with a straight face. All these contradicting sets of absolutist rules, with their insulting accusations about anyone making exceptions, while ignoring the exceptions taken by the “in” producers. The BS and insulting declarations came from writers and importers, self-serving and ignorant. Once people got answers from the producers they were touting, all we heard was pragmatism. Those top producers were accomplishing a goal, not limiting themselves with ill-informed dogmatism.

So, you talking to producers who source from one or a few vineyards, and successfully have the results is not near as informative as you think. They could not translate their practices to every worthy vineyard. I bet none of them has made natural-ish wines from as many regions (12?) or grape varieties (45?) or vineyards as we did. Like I said already, some natural ferments just don’t go well. Sometimes that is consistent from a site, something inherent.

One practice some people do is start naturally, then inoculate later, for whatever reason. As far as isolated strains go, some are chosen for the character they impart (usually from a well regarded vineyard), some are selected by their ability to meet a challenge (such as low nutrient level, ability to rapidly complete, higher than usual ethanol tolerance, etc.), some are selected for being “neutral” (meaning they don’t impart much character to the wine). In these cases, the wines get the complexity the wild stuff contributes. Full stop. Then they’re controlling the rest of the process, for various reasons. Varietal typicity. Avoiding flaws. Avoiding a stuck fermentation. In my experience, that hasn’t been necessary 95% of the time, but 95% isn’t 100%, and some people will just have a problematic vineyard or facility and need to intervene often or always. (And, as someone else pointed out above, I guarantee people who take on the “natural” moniker and claim to never add anything do add things, when necessary to save a wine. They may have gone in with good intentions, but aren’t going to flush tens of thousands of dollars of juice down the drain over dogmatism.) (PS. I’ve also seen interviews by unfortunate natural wine writers who unwittingly coerce winemakers to go along with a lie about their practices. Are they supposed to pipe up with an inconvenient truth, offend that writer and lose a proponent for their wines?)

Sorry about reality.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Depends what you’re selecting for.

The layer of bloom (mainly fatty acids) on the surface of the grape only forms at véraison - that’s to say only a few weeks from harvest. It is composed of around 75% molds (that cause decay, not fermentation) and only 25% yeast. Of that 25%, around 95% are apiculate yeast incapable of fermenting a wine to dryness (to say nothing of their frequent tendency to produce acetate). Within the remaining 5%, you will find only a couple of strains of saccharomyces cerevisiae. The actual composition of this bloom will be heavily determined by the weather (how much rain and what temperatures) from véraison on, as well as what strains happen to be blowing around; and far from being optimized for each vintage, the hotter and drier your conditions the more likely you are to have problematic microflora. Winemaking with native yeasts is about manipulating the parameters to select a very small number of strains from among the very many. The available parameters are temperature, use of sulfites, and presence of absence of oxygen (also pressure, in the case of sparkling wine production). But ipso facto all winemaking uses selected yeasts, whether selected from the many in the vineyard by choices in the winery, or selected in a laboratory; and for me the idea that weather-contingent microflora only present for a few weeks of the year is the ineluctable vector of terroir is one of the more stupid ideas in the contemporary wine world.

One of the major issues in the old natural wine debates was the glaring exceptions allowed that were specific to challenges of a given producer/region, stated with a straight face. All these contradicting sets of absolutist rules, with their insulting accusations about anyone making exceptions, while ignoring the exceptions taken by the “in” producers. The BS and insulting declarations came from writers and importers, self-serving and ignorant. Once people got answers from the producers they were touting, all we heard was pragmatism. Like I said already, some natural ferments just don’t go well. Sometimes that is consistent from a site, something inherent.

One practice some people do is start naturally, then inoculate later, for whatever reason. As far as isolated strains go, some are chosen for the character they impart (usually from a well regarded vineyard), some are selected by their ability to meet a challenge (such as low nutrient level, ability to rapidly complete, higher than usual ethanol tolerance, etc.), some are selected for being “neutral” (meaning they don’t impart much character to the wine). The misperceived is the same here. The fact that winemakers select from the yeast in their vineyards doesn’t change the source of the yeast and that source differentiates it from yeast coming from elsewhere, regardless of the issue of human selection, which is really an entirely separate matter. The distinction between using ambient yeast and inoculated yeast really has nothing to do with whether human beings are engaged in one practice or another. Whether that should be a criterion of terroir is a question of one’s definition of terroir. I suppose, by your definition, whatever it is, it could be seen as a stupid idea. By others, not so much.

I am the last person you’ll find who’ll undervalue the importance of agronomy!

However, in my view its biggest contribution to wine quality is by producing balanced, concentrated fruit rich in aroma precursors. This, indeed, is the strongest link between farming and fermentation outcomes, since balanced musts with healthy pHs select for more favorable microflora.

However, no matter the health of your vineyard and quality of your farming, some sites are more likely to be infested with brett, or susceptible to rot and concomitant acetic rot, for example, than others. So the question of whether those microbes are transient, weather-dependent potential pests, or the only route to making soulful wines of place, remains, and I’m very clear about how I would answer it.

In terms of protecting wine, there are a few methods worth putting in a cellar long term, imo:

1 low pH (<=3.3 pH, whites mostly),
2 moderate or lower (<=3.45) pH and good tannin structure (reds of course),
3 made in a meticulously clean cellar, or
4 sterile filtered wines

Option 3 needs a respectable amt of sulfur, according to its meticulous scale
2 & 4 needs a bit.

Option 1 is a good definition of natural wine, including without sulfur, and was much easier to make several decades ago, unfortunately too many use option 3 for nat wine without enough meticulous or sulfur

posts.name: David Patte, Sun Break Wines
posts.cooked:
This post is worth highlighting again. Exactly right. Yeast cells are dormant or only slowly reproducing without a bit of water on the surface of the grape (from rain but dew helps too). If the berries are damaged, the population of endemic yeast and bacteria and other microflora cells will explode dramatically. I found one study that showed S. cerevisiae is usually absent or rare in healthy grapes even in long-established vineyards (one healthy berry in one thousand carries wine yeasts). However on surface-damaged berries, the frequency rises to one in four, and populations range from 100,000 to 1,000,000 cells per berry. That said, populations of other organisms will also explode (some spoilage organisms, some benign). Wine makers tend to get a little nervous with damaged fruit. This is from Mortimer and Polsinelli, 1999, On the Origins of wine yeast, Res. Microbiol. 150, 199-204

On some of the other responses about the prevalence of a winery house yeast, which may have commercial, or off-site origins, there’s a budding new group of labs that can analyze this and let you know what species are in your fermentations, and whether they are unique or endemic to your vineyard or perhaps your winery. The natural ancestral habitat for S. cerevisiae is oak trees/habitats so it’s entirely feasible to have an endemic species close to the winery that is not found in the vineyard if the two are far from each other (yeast cells do not magically blow in the wind, and are slow to spread). And certainly if a winery were to purchase a used press or other used equipment, these can also be sources for spontaneous fermentations and become the dominant house fermentation species.
